sentences.text: My name is Joseph Sterngast.
I am born in 1913, 27 of August.
Today, I am 81.
I was born in Poland, in Krakow.
Yeah, I was born in Krakow, and I went to school in Krakow.
And I also was educated in Hebrew and Jewish in Krakow.
And I-- my-- I was at home.
We were six brothers and two sisters.
Unfortunately, today, I have only one brother left what he lives in Israel.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
How big.
That was 300,000 population.
And I was with my parents till the bar mitzvah.
After, a year after bar mitzvah, my mother passed away, and two years later, my father passed away.
So we were orphans.
We had to start to worry about ourselves.
And the elderly brothers and sisters, they do a lot for us, the younger-- the younger [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] brothers and the younger-- the younger brothers.
And the parents, they left our own house.
We used to live in our own house, and we had a store.
But one of my brothers, he had a printing business.
And me, when I was [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] 16, I decided to go and to learn a profession.
So I went to a school for almost a year to learn as a auto mechanic and auto driver.
What my-- special one sister was not very happy, that it's not the right profession for me, but I did it anyhow.

What I was very happy, what I did, then I find out in the wartime, because this was-- this was the only way that I survived, because I was very well known even at the Germans as a good mechanic, and I was working for them all the time.
And then when it started the war, they made a ghetto, not far from the place where I used to live, you know, almost on the same street.
And they closed up the ghetto.
And the permission to enter the ghetto was only for the people what they had a profession, and young people, they were able to work for the Germans.
And I was chosen.
I was one of them, between 1,000 of under.
They let in, into the ghetto.
And right away, I had a job in the Wehrmacht, [NON-ENGLISH] they used to call.
And I was working there because I was one of the good workers.
They put me as one of the responsible ones.
We were 103 people used to go out-- that was out of the ghetto.
And we used to walk quite a bit to the place where we were working.
And there, they used to give us a slice of bread and something to drink.
That's all what we got from them.
But the work was hard work.
That was over 50 years ago.
The tools, they didn't have those what the people work today.
Everything we had to do with our own hands.
And it was very hard, you know, to make an overhaul on the big engines, on the diesels, the Mercedes, and the NAG.
So-- but I was working, and they were very satisfied from me.
Very bitter was the life in ghetto, because--
Oh, before?
You mean when the war started or before the war?
Oh, when the war started it was--
Before the war, so I was at home, you know?
As we were orphans, we were all together.
And one of my oldest brothers, he left for Palestine.
So we-- the-- we, at home, we had to worry, you know, about our life.
How to-- so my sister, she took over the business, and that was the way we made a living.
It was grocery and delicatessen.
Very, very much, all the time.
In Poland, we always feel a lot of anti-Semitism.
And closer to the war was even much worse.
The main streets, they were closed with a big-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] with-- that was a special kind of closing up the ghetto, you know?
It was-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] how you call this-- a big door, you know?
It was only the entrance for the street car.
They were allowed to go.
They were.
But the streets were closed.
We were-- we-- we did see the street cars or the trucks what were allowed to move there.
About what year did this first happen?
This has happened in 1940, end of '40, '41.
OK.
So [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I-- I was very much engaged with sport, special-- you know, we had the club, Maccabee a Jewish club.
And we used to go almost every day, you know, there to-- for exercises.
And I was with my friends.
In the beginning, we had bikes.
We start to go on bikes, and then we start to race on bikes.
Maccabee, I joined-- I joined, since my childhood, when I was 15, 16 till 20, 21.
I had to serve the Polish Army.
So in the Polish Army--
Yeah.
That's-- you know, as a Jew, and I was-- and my health position was very good.
So I had to go wherever they sent me, you know?
It was cavalry.
But I was, because I had my papers that I am a mechanic and a driver, so my chief, the chief, the Major of the [INAUDIBLE] where I was there, he took me right away.
He had a car, an old one.
And I fixed a little bit the car.
And I was, as a driver, working with him, going whenever he asked for.
And even so far we were, you know, in good terms.
He likes me.
And whenever he used to leave out of town, he gave me the car.
He said, you just keep the car and you're the responsible one.
And the army was very, very hard, you know, in the Polish Army, and special for Jews.
Matter of fact, that-- one of the Jews commit suicide.
You know, it was so hard because the anti-Semitism was also in the Army.
But because my boss was a Colonel, this was the main on the all, you know-- in the all [INAUDIBLE].
He was the-- the only one.
But being in one of the special was a Rotemeister.
He was inside the one.
But outside was the Colonel.
And because it started that time already that I find out that I did a very smart move that I learned this profession.
That's why I was, you know, I would say one only.
What I didn't feel so much, you know, this anti-Semitism.

You know, when my Colonel used to go out of town, so my Rotemeister, the inside chief, was with his wife and children, he was coming to me, begging me if it's possible I shall take him to the hospital with the children.
And that's why I always, even being in the Army, I was a free man.
You know, whenever I wanted to be at home, even dressed as a soldier, I used to come for, you know, lunch at home, and for the weekends I had free when the Colonel didn't need me.
And others, you know, had very, very hard work.
It was with horses.
They used to go at night to work in the stables, you know, to clean up from the horses.
And I didn't feel it, this, you know, because I was special.
I was really like a chosen one.
For then, that time, somebody, a mechanic, an auto mechanic and a driver, was really, you know, very few.
So that was for three years I was in the Army.
In the Army, I got out end of '36, beginning of '37. 1937 I finished.
And I went back home.
And I wanted to move to Israel, and I had all my papers prepared.
But when they find out that I want to move to Palestine-- at that time, it wasn't Israel.
It was Palestine.
So they called me after a year, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] or after two years, they called me back for exercise, again, to the Army.
What I was supposed to go after three years, but they didn't want to let me out.
You know, it was because of anti-Semitism.
They didn't want, as a Jew, I should go to-- to Palestine.
Even they always used to say, Jews, they belong to Palestine, but if come the time somebody had the possibility to-- to go to Palestine, it was very hard.
So they took me to the-- for the exercises, you know, maneuvers.
And I was there for two months, again, as a driver.
And I didn't feel-- remember this was out of town, far away, you know, till after [INAUDIBLE], all the Polish states who used to-- they used to go on the horses.
And I used to go with my Colonel in the car, or a motorcycle with a bi-wagon.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]
So then they let me free.
I finished, but they didn't let me go.
And they start to talk about a war is coming that Hitler started.
People used to run away from Germany.
So the-- my brothers and one of the sisters-- because in meantime, one sister left for Palestine.
The brother, he called her out to Palestine.
So the sister with the children what was at home, and the bother, married with children, they was begging me, I shouldn't go.
I shouldn't leave them alone at home.
I shall stay with them.
So I decided I will be with them.
But it was a short time, because when Hitler came, you know, the Germans, they closed up everything, and they start to think about the ghetto.
So the rest of my family, they didn't have the chance to go into the ghetto, because their profession, they didn't like.
And they didn't give them the permission to be, to live into the ghetto.
So I was the only one.
And there, they gave me right away a job.
And I was working there for the Germans.
And I-- I didn't feel very hard for me because I used to come in the evening after the work.
So I was looking for something to eat.
And again, to go to sleep and to get up early in the morning.
And the soldiers, they were waiting for us, and took us out of town when we were working for them, you know, for-- for the Wehrmacht, for this Army, for the German Army.

That-- you know, it was allowed to go out of town, not in Krakow, far away from Krakow, because they gave them the permission to stay there for few months, because they knew they will get them all together in one place, or ten other places, and they will send them, you know, to kill them.
You know, it was prepared already, the camps.
They were killing the people.
And they killed my family what were there, you know, out of town you know?
And I-- from the ghetto, before they give-- they decided to kill the people, I didn't know, and I used to run away from the ghetto on a bike.
I had a bike with me in the ghetto.
No, bicycle.
And I used to visit them, like to say goodbye.
I didn't know what's gonna be.
But they told me I shall come there.
There is-- they don't see a soldier.
Until the time came, the soldiers came and take all of them, you know, with the children, with everybody.
They put them into the camps, and they-- they burn them, and they kill them.
Even now, the-- the-- the places where they did it, they are still not covered completely.
You still can find some, you know, leftovers from the killed people.
About Palestine, I knew that I had to work hard in Palestine, because the [INAUDIBLE], they were the-- above the Palestine.
They-- Palestine belonged, that time, to the-- to the English, you know?
And-- that-- I wanted to be with my brother and sister there, but then I gave up because it was impossible.
And I wanted, before they killed my family, I was-- I wanted to be with them.
But like I mentioned, they didn't let them go into the ghetto.
They didn't have the-- the profession.
And when the ghetto was finished, they start, again, to liquidate the ghetto.
1941. 1941, because I was in ghetto from 1940, 1941.
And then they start to liquidate the ghetto because it was a lot of people, too much of them.
The liquidation started-- they surrounded the whole ghetto with soldiers, all over.
No one can run away.
And they went into the apartments and they put the people on one place, and they start to kill them.
They were shooting without stopping, you know, parents with children, with grandparents, women, you know, they were, you know, with-- also, you know, they told it will be a regular life there.
So the women were pregnant.
So they killed everybody.
They didn't look.
But they choose because the soldiers came and they said that they needed people what they work for them.
And from the Wehrmacht, you know, from the Army, from the German Army.
So I had my 103 people.
And there were some other places where the Jews used to work.
Them they let out.
They didn't kill them.
And we didn't know what's going to happen because it was early in the morning.
We went for the whole day to work.
We came back from work, it was completely empty.
It was a few people only in the ghetto.
The left over was only the people what were working.
And-- That was all in one day that they killed everyone?
Oh, that was more than one day.
It was two days.
And they couldn't kill all of them in the ghetto.
So they were prepared with the big trucks.
And they used to send them to Auschwitz, and to Bergen-Belsen, to other places, you know, where they used to kill the people.
So not exactly all of them were killed there.
So they send out.
But this-- everything we-- those working people, they knew about that we-- we had, again, some papers from the Army that we are needed still.
But we presume that the end will be that they're going to kill us.
So then after the ghetto, they start to build concentration camps.
So also the concentration camp was not far from the ghetto.
They built on Jewish cemeteries.
That was two big cemeteries, because Krakow was like two cities.
Was-- and the Vistula was a bridge, was on the other side, that was Krakow.
And the other side from the Vistula after the bridge was [?
Podwoloczyska. ?] So I used to live in [?
Podwoloczyska. ?] So then they start to build the concentration camps.
They throw out all the people what were, you know, in the graves.
And my people, my parents, were buried in the same cemetery.
And when I-- they start to build the barracks.
So I was working on the barracks, too, there.
And I knew where my parents were buried, where we built the barrack.
So I knew that I am on the place where my parents were buried there.
And I was there in the camp for two years.
And like I-- you know, I-- I had in my mind that I am living with my parents.
You know, that was, the barrack was built on the graves.
So the life in the concentration camp was very bad.
Plaszow.
Yeah, straight from ghetto.
But still, they kept some people in the ghetto.
Also, people they had no special, you know, [NON-ENGLISH] that means profession.
They didn't have the right profession what they needed.
So again, they killed the people, and we were there in the camp.
